text: Evolution of dynamical networks enhances catalysis in a designer enzyme
H. Adrian Bunzel1,2 ✉, J. L. Ross Anderson1 , Donald Hilvert $\textcircled { 1 0 }$ 3 , Vickery L. Arcus4, Marc W. van der Kamp $\oplus 1 , 2$ and Adrian J. Mulholland  2 ✉

Activation heat capacity is emerging as a crucial factor in enzyme thermoadaptation, as shown by the non-Arrhenius behaviour of many natural enzymes. However, its physical origin and relationship to the evolution of catalytic activity remain uncertain. Here we show that directed evolution of a computationally designed Kemp eliminase reshapes protein dynamics, which gives rise to an activation heat capacity absent in the original design. These changes buttress transition-state stabilization. Extensive molecular dynamics simulations show that evolution results in the closure of solvent-exposed loops and a better packing of the active site. Remarkably, this gives rise to a correlated dynamical network that involves the transition state and large parts of the protein. This network tightens the transition-state ensemble, which induces a negative activation heat capacity and non-linearity in the activity–temperature dependence. Our results have implications for understanding enzyme evolution and suggest that selectively targeting the conformational dynamics of the transition-state ensemble by design and evolution will expedite the creation of novel enzymes.

ailor-made enzymes promise to be transformative for the ‘green’ synthesis of pharmaceuticals and fine chemicals, and the achievement of a circular economy1–3 . Increasingly, the de novo computational design of catalytic residues and ligand-binding pockets into protein scaffolds can afford incipient, yet generally modest, activity4,5 . Such ‘designer enzymes’ can be substantially improved by directed evolution, which may enhance activity by several orders of magnitude in the best cases6–11. This is now an effective way to develop new protein catalysts, but also demonstrates the limitations of current design protocols6 . Designed active sites often require fine-tuning by evolution to precisely position catalytic residues and tightly packed ligands7–11, increase electrostatic preorganization12–15, and stabilize catalytically superior conformations16–23. What is less clear is whether evolution exploits the scaffold’s dynamics to introduce a differential thermodynamic response to the transition state (TS).
Evolution of a negative activation heat capacity

The base-promoted Kemp elimination of benzisoxazoles is a valuable model for the catalysis of an elementary reaction24,25, and has become an exemplar for the designed biological catalysis of a non-natural reaction $^ { 9 - 1 1 , 2 6 , 2 7 }$ . We previously subjected the computationally designed Kemp eliminase 1A53-2 to directed evolution (Fig. $1 ) ^ { 1 0 }$ . Evolution boosted activity $1 0 ^ { 4 }$ -fold with the introduction of six mutations during optimization of the first-shell residues only. The binding pocket of the resulting evolved variant, 1A53-2.5, exhibited improved shape complementarity to a transition-state analogue by virtue of several space-filling substitutions10. The introduction of the A157Y mutation further enhanced activity by restricting the conformational freedom and tuning the $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathrm { a } }$ of the catalytic base Glu178.

Unexpectedly, activity gains in 1A53-2.5 coincided with the emergence of a curved activity–temperature dependence (Fig. $1 { \mathrm { c } } ) ^ { 1 0 }$ . This curvature is not due to protein unfolding or a change in mechanism (Extended Data Fig. 1)10. Instead, it can be attributed to an apparent activation heat capacity ( $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger } ,$ equation (1)) for the chemical step. There is increasing evidence that $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ is linked to thermoadaptation in natural enzymes due to its effect on an enzyme’s temperature optimum (equation $( 2 ) ) ^ { 2 8 , 2 9 }$ . Notably, directed evolution of 1A53-2 solely aimed to increase overall activity. The unexpected emergence of a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ suggests that this effect is more generally related to catalytic proficiency. A negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ indicates that energy fluctuations $\left( \Delta \langle { \delta \mathrm { H } ^ { 2 } } \rangle ^ { \ddagger } \right)$ are reduced in the transition-state ensemble compared with those of the enzyme–substrate complex (equation $( 3 ) ) ^ { 3 0 }$ , which potentially strengthens recognition of the chemical TS. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations can probe this thermodynamic effect and have yielded $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ values in good agreement with experiment for two natural enzymes28.

where $k _ { \mathrm { B } }$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ the temperature, $h$ the Planck constant, $R$ the gas constant, $\Delta H _ { T _ { 0 } } ^ { \ddagger }$ and $\Delta S _ { T _ { n } } ^ { \ddagger }$ the activation enthalpy 0and entropy at the reference temperature ${ \dot { T } } _ { 0 } ,$ and $T _ { \mathrm { o p t } }$ the temperature optimum29.
Here we show that the evolution of 1A53-2 reshaped its conformational dynamics, which drove the emergence of a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$

image_caption: Fig. 1 | Activity gains during directed evolution of 1A32-2 coincided with the emergence of a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ . a, The general base Glu178 deprotonates 6-nitrobenzisoxazole. b, Six mutations (blue spheres) were introduced during active-site optimization (ligand and base, orange carbons)10. c, The Eyring plot of 1A53-2 (red) is linear, whereas that of the evolved variant 1A53-2.5 (blue) is curved, which signals the emergence of a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ (ref. 10). Dashed lines indicate a linear Eyring fit, and solid lines show the macromolecular rate theory fit including $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ (ref. 29). Data extracted from Bunzel et al.10.

text: Strikingly, emergent correlated movements in the transition-state ensemble of 1A53-2.5 span the protein and link the active site with the protein scaffold through a dynamical network. This network enhances catalytic preorganization and helps to explain the role of distal mutations that arose during further evolution.

We performed $5 \mu s$ of MD simulations each for the designed and evolved variants in complex with either the substrate (Michaelis complex/ground state (GS)) or TS (Extended Data Figs. 2 and 3). The designed and evolved variants showed significantly different dynamical responses to the ${ \mathrm { T } } \mathrm { S }$ . The energy distribution of 1A53- 2.5 was notably narrower in the TS than in the GS. In contrast, no significant differences between the GS and TS were observed for 1A53-2 (Extended Data Fig. 4a,b and Supplementary Tables 1 and 2). The change in energy fluctuations between GS and TS determines $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger } ,$ which we calculate from a moving average over the simulations using validated methods (Extended Data Fig. 4c)28. Rigidification of the TS ensemble in 1A53-2.5 results in a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ $( - 1 9 . 7 \pm 3 . 1 \mathrm { k J } \mathrm { m o l } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { K } ^ { - 1 } )$ , whereas the activation heat capacity of 1A53-2 is zero within error $( 0 . 4 \pm 3 . 2 \mathrm { k J } \mathrm { m o l } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { K } ^ { - 1 } )$ . This is consistent with the experimentally observed linear and non-linear temperature dependence for the designed and evolved catalysts, respectively, and demonstrates the dynamical origin of $\Delta \dot { C } _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ .

The differences between the designed and evolved catalysts are significant, as demonstrated by the errors for $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ calculated by cross-validation, leaving out individual trajectories. Independent simulations using a different TS model in which the transferring proton resides on the base instead of the ligand gave similar results (TS2, see Extended Data Figs. 2–9). We calculated $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ directly from the difference in energy fluctuations between GS and TS, after removal of the bulk solvent. Our calculations predict a sizable negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ for the evolved protein—the magnitude is overestimated in part because protein solvation reduces $\breve { \Delta } C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ substantially28. Also, slow loop movements, which cannot be exhaustively sampled on a reasonable timescale, may mix into the fluctuations at longer window sizes. In short, comparison of the designed and the evolved catalysts shows that the non-linear temperature dependence of the evolved variant arises from dynamical differences between its GS and the TS complex.

The decrease in energy fluctuations associated with reaction in 1A53-2.5 is accompanied by reduced structural fluctuations in the TS ensemble (Fig. 2 and Extended Data Fig. 5). In 1A53-2.5, several solvent-exposed loops that cover the active site (residues 53–65, 84–92 and 181–192) are less mobile in the TS ensemble, as indicated by decreased root-mean-square fluctuations (∆r.m.s.f.) between the GS and TS. Cluster analysis (Fig. 2a and Extended Data Fig. 6) and principal component analysis (Extended Data Fig. 7) show that these loops interconvert between an open and a closed state. Both 1A53-2 and 1A53-2.5 have narrow substrate entry channels with bottleneck radii of $1 . 8 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ in their X-ray structures31. Although loop opening will facilitate ligand binding, several open–closed interconversions were observed for both variants (Extended Data Fig. 6i,j), which suggests that this motion is faster than turnover.

Loop closure is essential for catalysis in natural enzymes, such as triosephosphate and ketosteroid isomerase32–34 which catalyse proton-transfer reactions comparable with the Kemp elimination24,35,36. In these natural enzymes, loop closure desolvates the active site and improves preorganization32,33. Directed evolution of 1A53-2 similarily resulted in tight active-site packing shown by a decrease in solvent-accessible surface area of the ligand and base (Extended Data Fig. 8)10. Evolution (1) introduced space-filling mutations, such as A157Y and L184F, which led to better active-site packing, (2) removed water from the active site on loop closure and (3) shifted the open–closed equilibrium towards the closed state. Loop closure in the evolved variant furthermore results in a decrease of the protein radius of gyration (Extended Data Fig. 6), which suggests that pressure may also modulate fluctuations in $1 \mathrm { A } 5 3 - 2 . 5 ^ { 3 7 }$ . These effects—combined with the introduced hydrogen bond to Glu178 from A157Y—restrict the movement between the ligand and base (Extended Data Fig. 8) and preorganize the reactants for catalysis.

To understand how evolution affected TS recognition, interaction energies of individual residues with the TS were calculated (Fig. 2b). After evolution, several active-site residues interact more strongly with the TS. Notably, mutations introduced during evolution do not directly provide additional TS stabilization, but appear to indirectly strengthen interactions of the TS with the catalytic base Glu178, with Trp110 and Trp210 sandwiching the ligand, and with Arg182 stabilizing the nascent oxyanion by electrostatic interactions. This tightening is reflected by the ∆r.m.s.f. of most active site residues, which are less mobile in the TS than in the GS ensemble in 1A53-2.5 (Fig. 2b). These tight and ordered interactions enhance active-site preorganization; we show here that they are associated with the overall rigidification of the protein scaffold in the TS ensemble that causes the negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ .

image_caption: Fig. 2 | Global dynamics in the evolved enzyme are modulated by a local tightening of the active site associated with reaction. a, Distance-based cluster analysis of the closing loops (tubes) in 1A53-2 (red) and 1A53-2.5 (blue) reveals a conformational equilibrium between open (light colours) and closed (dark colours) states, as indicated, for example, by the $\mathsf { C } _ { \alpha }$ distance between residues 58 and 188 (spheres). The closed state becomes more populated after evolution (Extended Data Fig. 6). b, Evolution introduced six mutations at the active site (red or blue sticks, G211 as a sphere), which enhance the interactions of Trp110, Glu178, Arg182 and Trp210 (grey sticks) with the TS in the closed state. As a result, the closed TS ensemble of 1A53-2.5 is more constrained than its GS ensemble, as reflected by the reduced fluctuations (∆r.m.s.f.) of many active-site residues between the two states.

text: Dynamical correlations in the evolved TS ensemble connect local reorganization of the active site with the protein scaffold. The change in cross-correlations between the GS and TS was calculated for all backbone $\mathrm { C } _ { \alpha }$ atoms and the ligand, with the protein in the closed state (Fig. 3 and Extended Data Fig. 9). These correlations therefore reflect differences between the GS and TS, and do not involve conformational changes between the open and closed states. In the closed state of 1A53-2.5, large parts of the backbone move in a more correlated manner in the TS compared with that in the GS ensemble. Shortest path maps22 show that these increased correlations are communicated via neighbouring residues and indicate that evolution introduced a dynamical network that centres on the ligand and spans the protein (Fig. 3 and Extended Data Fig. 9). Only two mutations introduced during evolution are directly involved in the network. Q211G increases flexibility and potentially tunes the dynamic response of the scaffold, whereas L184F enhances packing by connecting neighbouring solvent-exposed loops. The other evolved mutations apparently facilitate the global dynamical response to the TS by tightening its interactions with native active-site residues (Trp110, Glu178, Arg182 and Trp210, as described above), rather than beeing part of the network.

1A53-2.5 was evolved by targeting only first-shell residues. Several distal mutations were identified during its further optimization by error-prone PCR, which provided an additional threefold activity improvement10. These mutations cluster around the differential cross-correlations in 1A53-2.5 (Fig. 3e,f), which suggests that evolution altered the dynamical network while slightly increasing activity. The limited improvements achieved during these last rounds of evolution indicate that further fine-tuning would require many more mutations to reprogram the global dynamics encoded in the scaffold. Although this may be challenging for laboratory evolution, nature probably evolved similar networks as indicated by sectors of spatially proximal coevolving residues38–40 with highly correlated dynamics41. Dynamical networks22,42–46 probably play a crucial role in enhancing preorganization, reducing non-productive conformations16–18,22 and tuning protein flexibility for thermoadaptation28,29,47. In contrast with other methods that often focus on a single state only, our differential analysis directly highlights networks involved in the protein response to the chemical TS. Targeting that response—by mutagenesis or computational redesign of key network vertices—may provide promising opportunities to tailor enzyme activity.

image_caption: Fig. 3 | Directed evolution introduced an extended correlated network in the TS ensemble. a,b, Evolution of 1A53-2 (red) (a) to 1A53-2.5 (blue) (b) increased the correlated movements in the closed TS ensemble. Cross-correlations that increase between GS and TS by $2 2 0 \%$ are indicated as lines on the structures (Extended Data Fig. 9). c,d, Shortest path maps22 in 1A53-2 (c) and 1A53-2.5 (d), calculated from the increased cross-correlations, demonstrate the evolution of a network centred on the chemical TS that spans the closed state of the protein. The sizes of the edges (black lines) and vertices (red and blue spheres, protein; orange spheres, ligand) indicate the strength of the network. e,f, The mutations in 1A53-2.5 (blue spheres), those found during further evolution using error-prone PCR (white spheres) and those in the final variant 1A53-2.9 (black spheres) cluster around the correlated movements (e) and networks (f, yellow mesh) in 1A53-2.5, which indicates fine-tuning of its dynamics by remote mutations.

text: The co-emergence of catalytic power and a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ raises the intriguing question of the connection between TS tightening (and the associated dynamical network) and catalysis48. Although deciphering this cause-and-effect relationship will require further research, exquisite catalytic preorganization certainly requires ordering at the active site14. The evolved 1A53-2.5 achieves improved catalysis by increased TS stabilization, and its negative $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ shows that the protein scaffold responds to the chemical TS with significantly altered dynamics. We note that protein dynamics do not ‘drive’ the reaction on the timescale of the chemical bond making and breaking. This is shown by our quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics umbrella sampling simulations that reproduce the improvements achieved during evolution within a TS theory framework (Extended Data Fig. 10). Instead, effective TS stabilization by exquisite active-site preorganization is a function of the cooperative response of the whole protein scaffold. The transition from a flexible GS to a rigid TS could thus lead to activity gains, albeit at the expense of introducing curved temperature dependencies and therefore a loss of activity at elevated temperatures. $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ is apparently a signature of the superior catalytic efficiency achieved by the more ‘enzyme-like’ 1A53-2.5. Furthermore, $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ signals the ability of the scaffold to adapt to the chemical TS, and may be an important feature for enzyme evolvability.

The dynamical network in 1A53-2.5 emerged on introducing only a few active-site mutations, at positions that are typically targeted by current design algorithms. Supplementing conventional design and directed evolution approaches with MD analysis of the most promising candidates will probably allow reliable in silico screening for catalytically superior variants. Specifically, we propose to analyse the dynamical differences between the TS and GS ensemble to identify variants that tightly recognize the chemical TS. The dynamical analysis presented here can be performed within days to weeks, on timescales compatible with practical engineering strategies in the laboratory. The emergent TS networks may also be exploited to guide the evolution of distal mutations by suggesting mutagenesis hotspots and targets for computational redesign49. Rapid approximate methods to model protein dynamics50,51 could, furthermore, reveal fold-specific dynamics52–54 to inform design and evolution.

Combining existing design algorithms with atomistically detailed $\mathrm { M D } ^ { 2 8 }$ simulations may help attain tight communication with the scaffold’s dynamics and the exquisite precision required to achieve excellent TS complementarity and catalytic preorganization.

Our MD simulations and statistical thermodynamic analysis demonstrate how the evolution of catalytic activity shapes protein dynamics. The catalytically superior 1A53-2.5 uniquely shows a correlated network and reduced fluctuations in the TS ensemble, which gives rise to a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ . Our findings suggest a connection between the cooperative response of the protein scaffold and TS stabilization at the active site. The results altogether imply that $\Delta C _ { \mathrm { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ may be an indicator of successful catalytic preorganization, and perhaps relates to evolvability. Enzyme catalysis is based on selective TS stabilization; targeting the scaffold’s dynamical response to the chemical TS by design and evolution may be essential to create artificial enzymes that truly rival their natural counterparts.

image_caption: Extended Data Fig. 1 | The emergent $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ in the Kemp eliminase variants predominantly reflects effects on the chemical step. a, In the activity-temperature dependence of 1A53-2.9, deviations from the linear Eyring behaviour are slightly more pronounced for $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ than $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \scriptscriptstyle M }$ . This indicates that the $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ for $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ is of similar magnitude to that of $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { M } ,$ though no precise $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ has been determined for $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ . Thus, the experimentally determined $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ of $- 1 . 1 7 \mathsf { k J } \mathsf { \bullet m o l } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { K } ^ { - 1 }$ for $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { M }$ predominantly reflects thermodynamic differences between the TS and GS ensemble. b, Comparison of the activity-temperature profiles and CD melt curves of 1A53-2 (red) 1A53-2.5 (blue) and 1A53-2.9 (black) shows that the apparent $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ is not due to thermal denaturation of the protein. The melting temperatures of all variants are high, and those of the evolved variants are significantly above their catalytic temperature optima. 1A53-2.5, which evolved an activation heat capacity, has an even higher melting temperature than 1A53-2 ( $8 2 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C$ vs. $7 5 ^ { \circ } C )$ . Also, long incubation of all variants at the highest experimental assay temperatures does not affect activity, which shows that there is no significant unfolding at this temperature on the timescale of the experiments. Of note: the Kemp elimination only involves a single deprotonation step. A change in mechanisms is neither likely, nor did we observe any kinetic signatures signalling rate-limiting substrate binding or product release (e.g. burst phases or hysteresis under single turnover conditions). All data presented in Extended Data Fig. 1 were extracted from Bunzel et al. JACS 2019.

text: Extended Data Fig. 2 | MD parameters and restraints. a, Ground state and b, transition state distances (red) and charges (black) based on gas-phase calculations with $^ 6$ -nitrobenzisoxazole and acetate. To prevent artificial rigidification of the transition state by introduction of a covalent bond between the ligand and catalytic residue,1 the transition state was simulated with the transferring proton residing either on the ligand (TS) or base (TS2). Both transition state models provided similar results, supporting the significance of our findings. \*, Because Glu178 has only two $\gamma$ -hydrogens, their charge is adjusted to the given value according to the corresponding three hydrogens in acetate. c, Similar to previous work,1 restraints were used to avoid drifting away from reactive conformations during the extended simulations. Weak harmonic restraints for the ligand-base distance $\pmb { \delta } _ { \mathsf { c - C } }$ (black, ${ > } 5 . 0 \AA .$ $5 . 0 \mathsf { k c a l m o l ^ { - 1 } } \mathring { \mathsf { A } } ^ { - 1 } \big )$ and the ligand-base dihedral angle $\Psi _ { 0 - \mathsf { C } - N - 0 }$ (red, $> \pm 6 0 ^ { \circ }$ , 5.0 kcal mol−1  rad2 ) were added to keep the ligand in the active site. Furthermore, the $\chi 2$ dihedral angle of Trp110 $\Phi _ { \mathsf { C - C - C - C } }$ (blue, $6 5 ^ { \circ } > \Phi _ { \scriptscriptstyle \subset \cdot \subset \cdot \subset \cdot \big } > 1 5 5 ^ { \circ }$ , 5.0 kcal mol−1  rad−2 ) was restrained, preventing its indole sidechain from blocking the active site. To avoid biasing the $\Delta C _ { \mathfrak { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ calculations, the same restraints were added to all variants and ligands.

image_caption: Extended Data Fig. 3 | MD trajectories. Individual (light colours) and average (dark colours) trajectories of 1A53-2 and 1A53-2.5 with GS (red), TS (blue) and TS2 (grey) bound. a, Based on the r.m.s.d, the first 50 ns of each simulations were discarded to avoid biasing of the analysis to the starting structures (dotted lines). The ligand-base distance $\delta _ { \mathsf { C - C } } ( \boldsymbol { \mathsf { b } } )$ , and dihedral angle ${ \Psi } _ { \scriptscriptstyle { 0 - C - N - 0 } } ( { \pmb { c } } )$ , remained within the weak harmonic constraints (dotted lines) for most of the simulations. For a definition of the restraints see Extended Data Fig. 2.
Extended Data Fig. 4 | See next page for caption.

text: Extended Data Fig. 4 | Changes in energetic fluctuations give rise to an activation heat capacity $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger } .$ . a, The energy distributions for 1A53-2 (TS: dark red; GS: light red) are almost identical, indicating a $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ close to zero. Energetic fluctuations were calculated after removal of all but 10 water molecules, though removing these only slightly affected $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ . b, The distribution for 1A53-2.5 is narrower for the TS (dark blue) than for the GS (light blue) complex, resulting in a negative $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ . Distributions in (a) and (b) are normalized to a 50 ns moving average and plotted against a log scale to emphasize changes in variance. c, $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ was calculated based on the difference in variance between the TS and GS ensemble for moving windows of various sizes, and converges after 50 ns to negative values for 1A53-2.5 (blue). For 1A53-2, $\Delta C _ { \mathsf { p } } ^ { \ddagger }$ does not significantly deviate from zero (red). Error bars were obtained by leave-oneout cross-validation. As expected, the statistical errors increase with larger window size, because slow conformational changes that are not exhaustively sampled begin to mix into the calculations, complicating the analysis1 . Clearly, the energetic fluctuations in 1A53-2 and 1A53-2.5 are different, which gives rise to the dynamical network that tightens the TS ensemble in the evolved variant1 . $\mathbf { d } + \mathbf { e }$ , Heat capacities calculated under various conditions qualitatively agree well with each other. Heat capacities were calculated either dry (e), or with the 10 water molecules closest to Glu178 bound (d) based on the difference in energy variance of the transition and ground state for running windows with varying size (5 ns to 80 ns) for 1A53-2 (red) 1A53-2.5 (blue) for TS (filled circles) and TS2 (open circles).

image_caption: Extended Data Fig. 5 | Structural fluctuations in 1A53-2 and 1A53-2.5. Residues that rigidify (blue) or become more flexible (red) between $a + b$ , GS and TS, and $\pmb { c } \mathrm { + } \pmb { \mathrm { d } }$ , GS and TS2. In 1A53-2.5, rigidification is particularly evident in three solvent exposed loops (residues 53-65, 84-92, and 181-192), which is reproduced by clustering based on the whole protein backbone. These loops were thus employed to cluster the structures in an open and closed state (Extended Data Fig. 6).

Extended Data Fig. 6 | Cluster analysis reveals an open-closed equilibrium. Average structures of the a, 1A53-2 and b, 1A53-2.5 ensembles in the open (light blue) and closed (dark blue) state in complex with the TS. Distance-based clustering was performed using the $\mathsf { k }$ -means algorithm based on the three solvent exposed loops (bold tubes, residues 53-65, 84-92, and 181-192). Loop closure is indicated by the $\mathsf { C } _ { \alpha }$ distance of residues 58 and 188 (spheres). ${ \pmb { \ c } } + { \pmb { \triangle } } ,$ Histograms and $\bullet + \dagger ,$ distributions of the open (light colours) and closed (dark colours) states bound to GS (red), TS (blue) and TS2 (grey). ${ \pm h }$ Loop closure decreases the radius of gyration which is smallest in the closed TS of 1A53-2.5 (blue line). $\mathbf { i } + \mathbf { j } ,$ The open and closed state interconvert on the timescale of the simulation. Error bars represent the standard deviation of 10 independent simulations.

Extended Data Fig. 7 | Principal component analysis reproduces the two state model. Principal component analysis of the mobile loops (residues 53-65, 84-92, and 181-192) was performed for the a, GS (red), b, TS (blue), and c, TS2 (grey) ensembles of 1A53-2 (left) and 1A53-2.5 (right). The histogram of the first principal component after partition of the trajectory according to the cluster analysis (Extended Data Fig. 6) into open (light) and closed (dark) states reproduces the two-state model.

Extended Data Fig. 8 | Local structural effects at the active site. $a + b$ , The distance-distribution of the ten water molecules closest to the $\mathsf { O } _ { \varepsilon }$ of Glu178 is shown for the GS (red), TS (blue), and TS2 (grey) in the closed (dark colours) and open (light colours) state. The active site becomes less hydrated in the transition states compared to the ground state for both enzymes. Furthermore, the A157Y mutation in 1A53-2.5 displaces a water molecule in the evolved variant at approximately $3 \mathbb { A } .$ . Remarkably, loop closure expels water from the active site in 1A53-2.5 (arrows) which was not observed for 1A53-2. The $\ c + d ,$ SASA of the ligand and $\bullet + \hphantom { i } \hphantom { i } \hphantom { \hphantom { 0 } }$ , RMSF of the ligand and base are furthermore lower in 1A53-2.5 than 1A53-2, indicating tighter packing of the ligand. Error bars represent the standard deviation of 10 independent simulations.

Extended Data Fig. 9 | Emerging dynamical networks in 1A53-2 and 1A53-2.5. Only dynamical changes between the same states are comparable. The comparison of 1A53-2 with 1A53-2.5 is either performed between GS to TS (left) or between GS to TS2 (right). a, Backbone movements become highly correlated in the closed TS ensemble of 1A53-2.5, indicating the presence of global vibrations comprising most of the protein. b, Cross correlations that increase between GS and TS by $2 2 0 \%$ are indicated as lines on the structures. c, Based on the cross-correlations, shortest path maps were calculated2 . These show that evolution introduced a communication network in 1A53-2.5 that originates from the TS and comprises large parts of the closed state. The size of the edge (black sticks) and vertices (blue spheres: protein, orange sphere: ligand) indicate the increasing weight of the network between GS and TS.

Extended Data Fig. 10 | QM/MM modelling of the Kemp eliminases. Free energy surfaces of 1A53-2 (top) and 1A53-2.5 (bottom) at the a, PM6/ CHARMM36, b, AM1/CHARMM36 and the c, PM6/AMBERff99SB level of theory. The ligand as well as the carboxylate of Glu178 were part of the QM region during umbrella sampling of the C-H and N-O bonds. The transition state (black dot) reproduces the asynchronous Kemp elimination with C-H cleavage preceding N-O cleavage observed for similar systems.3-5 PM6/CHARMM36 gave activation energies of 101 kJ∙mol-1 and 90 kJ∙mol-1, which reproduce the improvements achieved during evolution (86 and 68 kJ∙mol-1 for $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } )$ . Similarly, simulations with AM1/CHARMM36 preserved that trend, but gave higher energies (117 and 105 kJ∙mol-1). In contrast, simulations with PM6/AMBERff99SB resulted in an inverse trend for the activation energies (111 and 116 kJ∙mol-1). This observation for AMBERff99SB mimics the classical MD, in which the ligand was expelled from the active site within 100 ns.
